item.content:encoded: wait no fuck oh shit oh god oh damn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

What would happen if the red team wins?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will be honest. Without the spoilers, I would have no idea what was going on in this document :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers

+ show block
– hide block

from what I can tell scp-5370 was created by the foundation as a way to make sure that any knowledge foundation personnel have is not lost upon death. Specifically it works by capturing the souls of every foundation member upon death and stores them in the “black room”. That’s what RED TEAM is, a gestalt entity composed of the souls of countless foundation members. The chess game is a method of communication designed by the foundation to allow them to communicate with RED TEAM without the transfer of any cognito/infohazardous information. As for the weird ritual stuff they’re doing with the “miscellaneous human materials” and the whole eyeball-eating thing I’m guessing that’s part of the ritual needed to keep the game going and/or possibly a way of divining the most suboptimal moves to make

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely adore this! You did a great job at giving just enough information to make things evident without being obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good read, many of the SCP-5000 entries are overly long. This one has a good lenght but manages to be very impactful - feels very classic as well.

+1
Pawn takes king.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Being a new Ethics Committee member must really suck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could someone explain this to me as if I were a low IQ, 10 year old child, cause I read the whole thing three times, while being able to understand every word, but I still have no idea what this is.
Please and thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely no one:
AS: Shut up and eat the eyeball.

Upvoted, I enjoy a good chess SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Chessland was a former contest entry that was basically from what I remember a sequel to 1875. Was for the horror contest I believe. Pictures were interesting.

But you are right, it did not end up surviving the votes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hot damn. Way to give us just enough information to make this feel like a full story without giving us any goddamned information at all. XD Thanks goodness for the good guesser upthread. I do dearly love how mystery is an inherent part of this piece, not just a way in which it's written.

At the risk of bringing up an issue that's already been dealt with, I could swear there was already a skip called Chessland? Though maybe I'm thinking of an entry in a previous content, because I also seem to recall it was bad and got delet.
MMMM i love chess i play chess wayyyyy tooooo muchhh so this was a awsome scp maybe one of my favorites i would love if there were more chess scps

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I like it, but I can't help but wonder about the logistics of it all. For example, how are people added to the board, what does a chess-based encryption algorithm even look like, and how is it more memetically-secure than other (simpler) methods? Also, what happens if the program crashes? I understand that answering all of those types of questions would probably be a bit boring or unnecessary for most people, but I would personally be more interested in the SCP if they were.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My review: Spoilers
Oops all hell

Oops the foundation sends literally everyone to hell. At least that's the way I read it? It reads to me that most of the foundation would go to hell because of the horrible things they do in the name of normalcy (I tend to headcanon the SCP foundation as being overall lawful good and most of the people working there to, regardless of what they do, be in the right for the most part, but that's neither here nor there, since this isn't my headcanon.) and due to this ritual, literally everyone who ever works for the SCP foundation goes to hell eventually, though they get to spend a little while in a game of chess first in order to pass on information. (Though even 260,000 years of chess is 0% of eternity in hell. How the ethics committee okayed that is beyond me)

I liked this one a lot, I think it was a very interesting concept that takes the idea of a game of chess with death and twists it around on its head, and then crushes that head in gory vivid imagery. I wouldn't call it my number one pick for SCP-5000, but it's definitely up there, and I've happily +1'd.

Here are some less serious thoughts about this.

more spoilers
ooooh spooky

It makes me sad to think of all the good people at the SCP foundation that would have to spend eternity in hell simply because they got a job and tried to make the world a better, safer, more normal place. And all the janitors, they're personal too ya know.

I wonder if the foundation plans on using that SCP that was a portal to hell to try and get them out after all that time. I imagine 260,000 years later the foundation might finally have a way to get stuff out of hell.

The SCP foundation pulling people from hell to work for them again actually sounds like a good story, hmm.

Also, if this wins, it'd be the third SCP-X000 to be thaumiel. Not a bad thing, just something I noticed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spooky, with a well paced build-up that feeds me just the right amount of hints and bits of color as it goes. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. I liked the tone of the original title.

item.title: That new title tho
item.content:encoded:
Finally got time to sit down and read some of the contest entries and I knew from how fast this article spiked after posting that I was going to be in for a treat. The mystery is wonderfully subtle (except for the bits where it is not, in which case we are given some potent gore) and the idea is up there with respect to creativity.

Adding on to the fact that this is some of the outright more evil takes on the Foundation, and this is a very compelling read.

+1 Taf. I'd +2 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh this is incredible

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: if chessland is so good how come there isn't a chessland 2

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because approximately two point nine umpteen people told me this shared a name with another proposal, here's a name change out of spite. Shine on, Stork, wherever you are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe “Zugpflicht” would work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the total canon, but I'm not fully satisfied with the texts and structures. For example, I don't think to put "Initial Project Proposal" at the last addendum is usual or proper for an SCP file - normally researchers write the introduction first and logs next. This makes me feel that you're deliberately creating a suspense atmosphere, as I knew nothing before I read Addendum-C and then went back to read A and B again. Also, maybe that's because I didn't have enough background Occultism knowledge, but I had no idea about the things in Addendum-B, like the fishing hook, before I checked the discussions. Sorry for my downvote; I may cancel it when the contest is over.

Update: Ok now I've read the declassificaion and I finally roughly understood it. Considering its theme "mystery", it is a nice entry. I've changed to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i drew it cause its rlly good hope you enjoy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That explains it.
Thanks for your reply :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block
  • Per my answer to the commenter downthread, the kids not growing back refers to the separation performed in the ritual. Amitha rips out the bit of the man's history that makes him a father of two; of course, without a biological father there are going to be… issues with the state of the kids. If you've seen Back to the Future that's a pretty good comparison point. His being 'a father of two' will grow back, but the kids themselves may have been retconned permanently
  • The fishhook is in-universe the tool used to separate the bits of someone's identity for them. Maybe it's an SCP, maybe it's the ritual that makes it magic. Out of universe, people use knives a lot in magic rituals and it was both a more novel and more disconcerting mental image to use a fishhook on someone than it is to stab them.
  • In-universe the ritual imbues the eyeball with whatever move would call out to their mark the most. Out of universe, it's a callback to prophetic traditions like Roman haruspicy and also an attention-grabbing piece of squick. Full disclosure, I thought the piece might get really dry without some living colour, but it's a totally valid critique to be turned off by that sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Ah, betrayal, not in five minutes… Now I get it. This is horrifying and wonderful.


+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all: Great read, really interesting idea, horrifying implications. Thanks for the great submission!
I was even a bit disappointed that I didn't get "BLUE Team clearance" at the end and get to keep reading - but I get why leaving things out adds to the mystery and impact of the text.

I agree with OP though: I was taken out of the suspense by the graphic language, and didn't fully understand it either… Maybe someone can explain this to me:

+ show block
– hide block

What was that about the kids not growing back, or the fishing rod? And why were they eating an eyeball? I'd be glad if someone could enlighten me. :)
The pawn and the king are in the same box.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

In my headcanon, the ritual works by separating abstract qualities from people, and by law of contagion separating the person they're looking for with those qualities from the gestalt. Amitha genuinely isn't bleeding blood — having a piece of your history ripped out leaves a very different kind of scar to having a piece of your body ripped out.

Still hurts like hell, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First SCP-1875 and then this… Both wonderfully horrific, and yet you did it the hard way, without jump scares or gory pictures that bring physiological discomfort.

+ one thing I don't get
– hide block

The ritual is supposed to help find the particular "coin" they're looking for and get answers. How does it actually do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The gory bit was a little out of left field, but otherwise this was a great piece of restrained horror whose terrifying implications unfold gradually. Amazingly well written, too. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A truly frightening piece. You nicely toe the line between revelation and ambiguity so that even though the core mystery is revealed by the end, there's enough we don't know that the central log keeps its peculiar horror and the piece overall is perhaps even more unsettling than it originally was. An easy +1; I hope this continues to do very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that was an unsettling read. You may have pulled a few too many punches in the sense that it takes a while to get it, but when you do, it's damn good. +1 my friend!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall I *really* like the main concept of this one, but I don't like the gore in Addendum SCP-5000-B. Seems completely unnecessary to throw in some implied child mutilation, when the core concept of this one is more abstract/conceptual. It distracts from the core tone and focus of the SCP and doesn't add anything to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Double bingo. Technically the original intent of the note about mortality rates was more a wink and a nod to the fact that BLUE TEAM membership also means you're keenly aware you are 100% going to hell when you die, but that interpretation also works too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since we're asking questions:

+ show block
– hide block

Am I right in thinking that the language about sending blue team members to advise red team is suggesting that they are executed (and thus added to the intelligences operating in the blackroom)? The note about extremely high mortality rates also seemed to suggest this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotcha!

+ show block
– hide block

Avoiding hell by playing Death at chess!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoilers
– hide block

Bang on the money.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, if I'm understanding this correctly (spoilers in the collapsible)…

+ show block
– hide block

RED TEAM is a gestalt of the bodies and souls of all Foundation personnel that have died since the blackroom's creation, that are trapped in a pseudo-purgatory until the blackroom is shut down or the game ends, which is when they all go to hell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading the comments, I suspect I'm missing the full implications here. I upvoted primarily because I thought it was horrific in a slightly wacky way (perhaps the over-the-top obtuseness of the whole approach to the problem?) - which I very much like!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2921

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the implications of that last bit are chilling. I loved reading this. that said, it didn't gut punch/wow me in a way that I expect from a winner of an -000 slot. nonetheless, this was a fun read and very well written;, I'll be sure to come back here and drop a +1 once the contest ends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from the existence of another article (SCP-2921) titled Zugzwang, I adore everything about this. It has a wonderfully subdued air to it; everything's understated and nothing's dwelled on for an obvious narrative effect, it's all just… there. It's a wonderfully horrific and creative anomaly, but the presentation here is so tonally consistent and well-thought-out that it could've been the shittiest anomaly in the contest and I'd still probably have upvoted for the quality of writin alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly incredible. Amazing job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite thus far. Interdimensional chess is already something I would love for the concept itself but the mystery of RED TEAM's nature is a great hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Elegant ballroom contest entry and possibly the only one I've liked so far. +1, even if it doesn't win it deserves to be a lore staple on par with Red Reality or Tabula Rasa.

edit: like jesus fucking shit this gives me uncomfy chills so bad my kids are gonna be born with tremors

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This strikes a perfect balance of clinical language and horrific subject matter. I deeply, deeply appreciate that there's no second iteration that's less redacted than the first. Everything here perfectly captures the spirit of the contest and is written with care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think we already have the title Zugzwang elsewhere, might have to change the title when the contest is over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think those above me really said it best. The stakes here are wildly high and while there is quite a bit left unanswered, any reader who was paying attention should come away from this article with enough information to get their mind jogging.

There's a balance between saying too much and not saying enough, and this toes that line perfectly.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This post is so much shorter than the rest, but that ending hits you like a truck.

Besides Tufto's behemoth of world-building, this was the first article I've read that I'm really enthusiastic about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I like this. I feel its pretty implicit what RED TEAM is by the end, but the subtle implications of the rituals and the implied fate once the game ends mixes with the confirmation in a great way.

I don't think I wanna know what RED TEAM knows that's so important doing this was considered ethical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first 5K entry that I adore unconditionally. The mystery is interesting and has a fantastic payoff, the chess game and associated rituals are gory without being gratuitous, the pictures make sense in and out of universe, and the whole article is delightfully understated. Stross himself couldn't write a better Laundry Files short story.

item.title: AIM FOR THE RIBS
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to ARD, Jekeled, oboebandgeek99, ch00bakka and minmin.

Who is RED TEAM?

Image credit:
- board is own work using a chess program and photomanip to turn the bishop upside down
- chess computer https://www.flickr.com/photos/pargon/2444943158 (ccbysa2.0)

Preferred slots: SCP-5370, 5565, 5585, anything divisible by 10. Make it a surprise, idc.

Edit: As the progenitor of the term 'blackroom' and a killer Cold War time travel podcast, please listen to Ars Paradoxica.
